responsibilities: Responsible for guiding with virtue
No fixed operational duties
Oversaw the affairs of the Masters of Writing (Lu Shangshu Shi) upon appointment at an emperor's ascension
historical_notes: Emperor Shizu appointed Zhuo Mao as Taifu; after his death, the position was abolished. Afterward, whenever a new emperor ascended the throne, a Taifu would be appointed to oversee Shangshu affairs, and upon his death, the position was again abolished.

responsibilities: In charge of military affairs and performance evaluations for all directions
Submits a report on the best and worst performers at the end of each year to execute rewards and punishments
Responsible for the second offering (ya xian) during suburban sacrifices
Announces the posthumous title at the southern suburb during a grand imperial funeral
Discusses major state constructions or uncertainties jointly with the Situ and Sikong
Jointly advises and remonstrates with the Situ and Sikong if the state commits a serious error
historical_notes: When Emperor Shizu ascended the throne, this position was known as Daisima (Grand Commander). In the 27th year of the Jianwu reign period (31 AD), it was renamed Taiwei.

responsibilities: In charge of overseeing the affairs of various departments (诸曹)

rank: Equivalent to 400 Shi, 300 Shi, or 200 Shi historically; generally considered 100 Shi later
responsibilities: Act as the functional administrative staff (gongfu yuan) of the Grand Marshal's office, divided across functional departments
historical_notes: According to the old Han system, Eastern and Western Cao yuans were ranked equivalent to 400 dan, other yuans as 300 dan, and shus as 200 dan (comparable to ancient yuanshi of three ranks). Early on, appointments were reported upward, giving them official standing. Later, officials directly appointed them themselves, and they became generally ranked as 100 dan.

responsibilities: In charge of managing clerks and appointments within the office
Oversees the promotion or removal of senior officials ranked at two thousand dan, as well as military officers
Responsible for civilian households, sacrificial rites, and agriculture and sericulture
Handles matters of submitting proposals and deliberations
In charge of handling lawsuits and disputes
Oversees postal relays, regulations, and scheduling matters
Responsible for managing convicts and transportation affairs
Handles matters related to bandits and thieves
In charge of legal judgments and penal matters
Oversees military affairs
Responsible for currency, salt, and iron matters
Oversees granary and grain affairs

title_en: Master of Records of the Yellow Chamber
responsibilities: In charge of recording and reviewing various administrative matters
Manage clerical records, attendants, and office logistics
historical_notes: According to the old Han records, clerks (gong lingshi) were ranked at 100 dan, but since the revival period onward, their official dan rank was no longer specified.

personnel_count.notes: Part of the 23 clerks and attendants

responsibilities: Responsible for serving as attendants to the Grand Marshal

personnel_count.notes: Part of the 23 clerks and attendants

responsibilities: In charge of matters related to ceremonial protocols and dignity within the office

personnel_count.notes: Part of the 23 clerks and attendants

responsibilities: Responsible for submitting memorials, reports, and official records

personnel_count.notes: Part of the 23 clerks and attendants

responsibilities: Oversees the gates of the office
Each manage the documents and records of their respective departments
In charge of managing the people and civil affairs
Oversees public education in filial piety, fraternal respect, humility, obedience, modesty, and frugality
Deliberates on regulations and establishes standards for nurturing life and handling funerals
Submits annual performance evaluations of civil affairs from all directions to execute rewards or punishments
Inspects sacrificial animals and ensures cleansing protocols are followed during suburban altar sacrifices
Oversees the safekeeping and placement of the imperial coffin during a grand funeral
Discusses major uncertainties or important state affairs jointly with the Grand Marshal
historical_notes: When Emperor Shizu ascended the throne, this office was known as Daisitu (Grand Minister of Instruction). In the 27th year of the Jianwu reign period (31 AD), the word 'Da' was removed, renaming it Situ.

title_en: Chief Administrator of the Minister of Instruction
responsibilities: Assists the Minister of Instruction in managing the affairs of the various departments and overseeing staff

title_en: Bureau Heads and Subordinates of the Minister of Instruction
responsibilities: Manage specific functional departments handling civil administration, education, and evaluations within the office
historical_notes: When Emperor Shizu ascended the throne, following the precedent of Emperor Wu, he established the position of Sizhi (Rectifier of State) within the Chancellor's office to assist in supervising and recording matters from various provinces. This position was abolished in the 18th year of the Jianwu reign period.

title_en: Clerks and Attendants of the Minister of Instruction
responsibilities: In charge of managing official records and documents of the departments, and serving as personal attendants
In charge of water and land affairs
Deliberates on benefits and establishes implementation plans for constructing cities, building towns, dredging ditches, and repairing embankments
Submits annual performance reports on water and land management tasks from all directions to carry out rewards or punishments
Responsible for cleaning and preparing musical instruments during suburban sacrifices
Oversees the final earth-covering and reburial of remains during a grand funeral
Advises and remonstrates jointly with the Grand Marshal (Taiwei) during significant constructions or uncertainties in state affairs
historical_notes: When Emperor Shizu ascended the throne, this position was known as Daisikong (Grand Minister of Works). In the 27th year of the Jianwu reign period (31 AD), the word 'Da' was removed, renaming it Sikong.

title_en: Chief Administrator of the Minister of Works
responsibilities: Assists the Minister of Works in overseeing the administration of various departments and staff within the office

title_en: Bureau Heads and Subordinates of the Minister of Works
responsibilities: Manage functional departments specialized in civil engineering, water conservancy, and performance evaluations

title_en: Clerks and Attendants of the Minister of Works
responsibilities: Responsible for administrative documentation, records processing, and acting as personal attendants

personnel_count.notes: Not a permanent position; appointed as needed

responsibilities: In charge of waging military campaigns against rebellion and betrayal
historical_notes: Originating in the Qin and Jin dynasties as a senior official title, it was not a permanent post in the Eastern Han. Generals were appointed for specific military campaigns and usually dismissed upon completion of their missions. Later, during periods of political decline, it became a regular capital appointment often given to consort clans.

personnel_count.notes: Ranked first among the four senior general ranks

responsibilities: In charge of major military campaigns and strategic defense
Remained in the capital to oversee military affairs during later periods
historical_notes: First created by Emperor Wu for Wei Qing with the title Daisima prefixed to rank above the Three Dukes. During the Eastern Han, its rank fluctuated from below the Three Dukes under Emperor Shizu, to above them under Emperor He, and eventually became fully equivalent to the Three Dukes as a permanent capital post for consort relatives from Emperor Shun's reign onward.

personnel_count.notes: Ranked second among the four senior general ranks

responsibilities: In charge of military expeditions and campaigns
historical_notes: Held by Jing Dan during Emperor Shizu's reign, ranked below the Three Dukes. Emperor Ming appointed his brother, Dongping Wang Cang, to this post, placing it above the Three Dukes due to his royal status, but it was abolished a few years later.

personnel_count.notes: Ranked third among the four senior general ranks

responsibilities: In charge of specific military expeditions and border defense
historical_notes: Appointed transiently for border campaigns; Ma Fang was appointed acting general to fight the Western Qiang under Emperor Zhang; Dou Xian held it to campaign against the Xiongnu under Emperor He; Deng Zhi held it to campaign against the Qiang under Emperor An. These posts were typically abolished upon the return of the expeditions.

personnel_count.notes: Ranked fourth among the four senior general ranks

responsibilities: In charge of military campaigns and guard operations
historical_notes: One of the four ranks comparable to the Three Dukes, temporary appointment for military necessities.

personnel_count.notes: Includes Front, Rear, Left, and Right Generals, and others

responsibilities: Command military campaigns; dismissed upon completion of their assignments
historical_notes: Numerous miscellaneous generals existed during the revival period under Shizu for military campaigns. Most had no permanent staff and were completely disbanded once peace was restored.

responsibilities: Guards against and monitors the newly surrendered Southern Chanyu and his followers due to suspicions of divided loyalty
historical_notes: Initially established by Emperor Ming as a temporary measure due to unrest among the Southern Chanyu's forces, but later converted into a permanent garrison command post.

responsibilities: Manages the overall administrative affairs of the General's office
historical_notes: Part of the permanent staff structure for established General offices.

responsibilities: In charge of military and operational matters, similar to the functions of the Grand Marshal (Taiwei)
historical_notes: The primary military executive within the General's central headquarters.

responsibilities: Participate in military deliberations, strategic planning, and advisory councils

title_en: Bureau Heads and Subordinates of the General
responsibilities: Staff the functional military administrative departments within the office
historical_notes: Includes specialized departments like the Military Affairs Bureau (Bingcao) and Rations Bureau (Binjiacao).

responsibilities: Responsible for managing military operations and weaponry equipment
Oversees the distribution of rations, leave requests, and enforces discipline and prohibitions

title_en: Clerks and Attendants of the General
responsibilities: In charge of clerical records, processing documents, and acting as attendants or guards
historical_notes: Completes the administrative civil staff of the General's office.

personnel_count.notes: Includes camp managers, guards, and judicial officers

responsibilities: Collectively manage camp logistics, security, and criminal law enforcement
historical_notes: Consists of specialized officers assigned to camp commands: Zhilijiubang (one per department to manage camp affairs), Woci and Cijian (criminal law), and 30 official horsemen with ceremonial drummers.

personnel_count.notes: Five Division Colonels under the General-in-Chief's camp

responsibilities: Commands one of the five major divisions (Bu) making up the General-in-Chief's field army
historical_notes: The General-in-Chief's central army was standardly organized into five divisions. If a division was not assigned a Colonel, it was instead commanded directly by a Military Sima.

personnel_count.notes: One per division; also acts as division commander if no Colonel is appointed

responsibilities: Serves as the executive officer and second-in-command of a division (Bu)
Assisted by a Military Adjutant Sima (Jun Jia Sima) as a deputy

personnel_count.notes: Number varies based on tactical needs

responsibilities: Commands a detached or independent unit outside the main five divisions; troop size varies depending on current circumstances
Commands a tactical unit called a Qu underneath a division
Assisted by an Adjutant Marquis (Jia Hou) as a deputy

personnel_count.notes: Assigned to division or camp gates

responsibilities: Responsible for guarding and managing security at the camp gates
Commands the lowest tactical subunit called a Chun underneath a Qu
